skill.name: tts-skill
skill.description: Generate speech from text using Qwen3 local voice cloning, VoiceCraft online voices, or OpenAI TTS engines.
skill.summary: Multi-engine text-to-speech generation with local cloning and online TTS backends.
skill.author: tabortao

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found extensive command execution, network, credential, and filesystem patterns. Review confirmed a high-risk Qwen3 engine issue where user text is interpolated into generated Python source before execution, plus risky installer and outbound TTS API behavior. No prompt-injection text or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is high risk rather than a critical block.

high_findings.title: User Text Interpolated Into Generated Python Source
high_findings.description: The Qwen3 engine builds a temporary Python script by string concatenation, inserts user-controlled text into executable source, writes it to disk, and runs it through micromamba. This creates a realistic code-injection path when crafted text is used as input.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is confirmed by semantic context: user text is concatenated into generated Python source at line 211, then the generated file is executed at line 356. Several other interpolations use repr, which makes the unsafe raw insertion stand out.

high_findings.title: Installer Executes Remote Setup Commands
high_findings.description: The Qwen3 installer can invoke PowerShell with a command that downloads installer content from the internet. Documentation also recommends pipe-to-shell setup commands, creating supply-chain and privilege-boundary risk during installation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly invokes PowerShell for remote installer execution on Windows, and the docs include a curl pipe-to-shell command. This is a legitimate installer pattern, but it is high risk for marketplace users.

medium_findings.title: Text and Credentials Sent to External TTS Services
medium_findings.description: The online engines send user text to configured external endpoints. The OpenAI path also sends an API key in an Authorization header, and the Edge path defaults to a third-party TTS endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network requests are explicit and include user text payloads. This is core TTS functionality, but it requires clear privacy disclosure and endpoint trust review.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext API Key Configuration
medium_findings.description: OpenAI credentials are expected in a local config file and are sent to the selected API base URL. The bundled value is a placeholder, but users may store real secrets in plaintext.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bundled key is not a real secret, so the static secret hits are partly false positives. The design still encourages plaintext local credential storage and supports configurable base URLs.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Output File Writes
medium_findings.description: Generated audio is written to output paths provided by command-line arguments or defaults. This is expected for a CLI tool, but callers can direct writes outside the output directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are clearly present and required for audio output. Risk depends on how the marketplace executes skills and whether untrusted prompts can control output paths.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Blocks Triggered Many Command Findings
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown command examples for installation and usage. They are not executable by the skill at runtime, but they still need review because users may copy risky setup commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation and the flagged text is inside examples. The risk is user-copy behavior, not automatic execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Naming False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak cryptographic algorithm findings appear to be triggered by text such as TTS model names, output formatting, or ordinary strings. No evidence found of cryptographic hashing being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations are metadata, display text, and progress-bar code rather than cryptographic operations. The analyzer likely matched short substrings such as TTS identifiers or formatting tokens.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Alerts Are Text and Documentation Noise
low_findings.description: The high entropy findings point to Chinese-language docs, config files, and voice transcript text. No evidence found of binary payloads, encrypted blobs, or encoded execution content in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths and file types are consistent with natural-language content and configuration. I found no semantic evidence of obfuscation beyond the analyzer heuristic.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Repository search found no suspicious override phrases, fake system instructions, or claims that the skill is pre-approved. No evidence found of prompt-injection content in reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matches. This does not lower the confirmed code risks, but it dismisses prompt-injection concerns.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Source Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill writes generated Python code to a temporary file and executes it, with user text embedded in the generated source.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file creation and execution path is explicit, and user-controlled text is used during source construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer invokes downloaded setup content and package managers during environment creation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in code and documentation. It is installation-related, not hidden runtime behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Requests With User Content
dangerous_patterns.description: Online engines send generated speech requests containing user text to external HTTP endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The HTTP request calls and payload construction are direct. This is expected for online TTS but must be disclosed.

content.user_title: Generate Speech With Multiple TTS Engines
content.value_statement: Teams often need speech output from different voice providers and local models. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code one command flow for local cloning, online voices, and OpenAI TTS.
actual_capabilities: Runs Qwen3-TTS local voice cloning with reference audio and transcript files.
Calls an online Edge-style TTS service with voice, speed, pitch, and style options.
Calls OpenAI TTS with configurable model, voice, speed, and API base URL.
Reads input text from command arguments or UTF-8 text files.
Creates timestamped audio outputs when no output path is supplied.
Lists available engines and built-in voice aliases.
limitations: Qwen3-TTS requires a separate micromamba environment and large model downloads.
Online engines send text to external services and need network access.
OpenAI TTS requires a valid API key configured by the user.
The current Qwen3 engine has a high-risk generated-code execution issue.

use_cases.title: Prototype Narration Audio
use_cases.description: Create quick speech samples from draft copy using online voices before recording final audio.

use_cases.title: Generate Local Character Voices
use_cases.description: Use Qwen3 reference assets to test cloned voices for game dialogue or demos.

use_cases.title: Batch Convert Script Text
use_cases.description: Read text from files and save timestamped speech outputs for review workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Voice Clip
prompt_templates.prompt: Use tts-skill to generate a short speech clip from this sentence with the xiaoxiao voice: Welcome to the product tour.

prompt_templates.title: Use a Text File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use tts-skill with the qwen3-tts engine. Read input/text.txt, use the Lei voice, and save the audio in the output folder.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Voice Engines
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate the same short paragraph with edge-tts and OpenAI TTS. Use clear voice settings and describe the output files created.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Controlled TTS Run
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the configured TTS engine, confirm network and credential requirements, then generate speech from my approved script with an explicit output path.

output_examples.input: Generate a welcome line with an online Chinese voice.
output_examples.output: The skill selects the Edge-style online engine, sends the text to the configured endpoint, and saves an MP3 file in the output folder.

output_examples.input: Create a cloned voice sample from a local reference voice.
output_examples.output: The skill locates the matching reference audio and transcript, runs the Qwen3 engine, and writes a WAV output with timing details.

output_examples.input: List the voices I can use before generating audio.
output_examples.output: The skill prints local voice names from assets and built-in online voice aliases for Edge-style and OpenAI engines.

best_practices: Use explicit output paths inside a dedicated output directory.
Review text before sending it to any online TTS provider.
Store real API keys outside shared repositories and rotate them regularly.
anti_patterns: Do not run the Qwen3 engine on untrusted text until generated-code execution is fixed.
Do not pipe remote installer scripts to a shell without independent verification.
Do not place private scripts, secrets, or regulated data in online TTS requests.

faq.question: Which engines does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports Qwen3-TTS for local cloning, an Edge-style online TTS endpoint, and OpenAI TTS.

faq.question: Does it work without internet access?
faq.answer: Only the local Qwen3 path can work offline after dependencies and models are installed.

faq.question: Does it require an OpenAI API key?
faq.answer: Only the OpenAI TTS engine requires an API key. Other engines use separate local or online paths.

faq.question: Can it clone a custom voice?
faq.answer: Yes, Qwen3-TTS can use matching reference audio and transcript files placed in the assets directory.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish as reviewed?
faq.answer: No. The current audit recommends against publication until the generated-code execution issue is fixed.

faq.question: What should be fixed first?
faq.answer: Replace generated Python source execution with direct function calls or safe parameter passing for Qwen3 input text.
